speaker: interviewer
text: Yaacov Rand.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I said, this is a long story. There are many other short stories that I didn't mention, like, for instance, that we were all-- we had all to-- to bear a yellow star. But this does not-- did not start in the-- in Transnistra. It started much earlier, even when we were home at the given stage. There was an order from the government that all of us, we had to put on a yellow star. And so there are many other little stories.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the star like? Did it have any writing?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Magen David.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any writing on it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was it attached?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother sew it to our-- to our coats.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to wear any armbands?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. So to come back to what we did with all such temperatures. So we naturally, wood became a very important thing. So where do you take wood from? Money to buy it, you didn't have. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we had still enough money to buy from somewhere an iron hatch, you call it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Hatchet.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hatcher. And we used to go in the night-- and I'm hope that the FBI won't come after me-- but to steal wood. We stole wood from all houses that were destroyed, either by the war or by the flood. Whatever was burning, it somehow disappeared. And two things I would like to tell you, concerning these nights of going to steal wood. One night, I went with my father, and we came on the shore of the [INAUDIBLE] to a building. And when we looked closer to the building, we saw that that was a synagogue before. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I asked my father, [NON-ENGLISH], is it permitted? So my father saw the wine, and then he said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. It's not that it's permitted. It's a commandment. And I understood immediately we are struggling for life. And my father who had such a long beard, a real Orthodox Jew, worked with his little hatchet, on the synagogue with tears in his eyes. But he did it in order to save the life of his-- his wife and his children and of all of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was patrolling or supervising him at this time? Did they notice you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Romanian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nobody noticed that you were missing at these times?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was no control. They didn't care if you were frozen or not. In the morning, the services of the [INAUDIBLE] used to collect all these bodies on wagons, very often dragged by themselves, had to bring them somewhere where they were buried. This is different. But before going into that matter, I would like to give you a second incident which was very meaningful to my life in the ghetto. We came to our house, and we went up to the attic. And we found a huge box, a wooden box, and as we opened up the box, we found inside books-- Hebrew books that one of the Zionists of those times has hidden in his attic under Stalin and under the-- the Communist regime and kept it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I found there [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE], which was a periodical, and [? Kalman ?] [? Schumann, ?] a lot of things, endless things. And that night, and I think one night more, in addition to wood, we stole books, and we bought those books to our little place in the [? casino. ?] And this became a source of life that you can't imagine. We read it. We re-read it. We shared with friends who know-- knew Hebrew-- who-- who knew to read Yiddish. And we had something spiritual with us. None of us carried books there. There might have been a Siddur or a [INAUDIBLE] or something, or a [INAUDIBLE], but that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have tefillin with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: 'Tillum'?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tefillin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Sure, we had. But before this, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] these books, when I went-- when we went home to Romania, already-- already on the Russian-- on the Russian domination, we came to the borderline. And we had there-- I had a bag with such books. And before going home, a good friend of mine in Czernowitz gave me a present-- a Hebrew, Hebrew dictionary made in Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came to-- there was a [INAUDIBLE] of the NKVD. The KGB of today was called at that time NKVD. And he found that my father had a shofar. The shofar, by the way, he bought on the market from a goy. So he asked him, what's that? He says, that's-- that's an instrument. What do you do with your instrument? You blow into it, and it plays. Let me see. He took, and he blew. Didn't work anything. So he asked my father, could you show me how you do it? So my father made [HEBREW] shofar. So he took it away. He came to the books. He asked what is here? I said, books. He opens up the bag and was this dictionary. He see-- he saw 'made in Palestine,' took away all the books. And then he came to the Sefer Torah. He said, what is that? So my father said, that's a holy book. He said, do we have dollars there? So my father said to him, Mr. [INAUDIBLE], if you would have had a holy book, would you put dollars in? He put the Sefer Torah back, and that's how I got out with it.

speaker: interviewee
text: The only book which remained from all these that we had there is a [INAUDIBLE] book with a [NON-ENGLISH], with an interpretation of the [HEBREW] which is called the holy [SPEAKING HEBREW]. And I-- it is a nowadays about 175 years old. I have it in my house. And on-- on the cover of it, my father put in all the [NON-ENGLISH of the family, and I added all those [INAUDIBLE] that were afterwards. That's the only thing which remains of those books, and I keep very, very dear to my heart.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any epidemics in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Immediately after we came, the living conditions were terrible, as I described to you. We were squeezed together. The water was frozen. By the way, there were some places of running water in the courtyards. And we used to go with some vessels to-- to bring water. And the Ukrainian, which were sometimes terrible, they used to chase us. There was a huge line. And then you came already close to the-- to the-- to the faucet. Then they had to go again into the line. So we used to go in the night when there was no lines. So it was terrible. And immediately, there were epidemics of it's called [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. I don't know how this is called in English. And these-- this typhus is brought over by lice. And because you--w e had-- all of us, we had lice, so there were a lot of people who fall-- fell ill. And grew-- and thousands didn't-- didn't make it. In my own family, we had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my grandfather, my mother's father, my grandmother, this Maurice Rath, who passed away in a period of about one month, all the three out typhus. They got contaminated from each other. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then later, we had already some medications possible to be-- to have.

speaker: interviewer
text: How'd you get them?

speaker: interviewee
text: By bribing officers-- well, all kinds of things. There were doctors who had them, some that they brought with them, some-- so there was a little bit of possibility to help out people who became sick. But it depended very much what kind of a heart you had. It was the heart-- the strength of the heart that determined if you survived or not. Those who were sick and survived, they were immunized-- immunized. And they could treat other people with-- with typhus. Otherwise, we tried not to get mixed with such people. Somehow, I treated a lot of people with typhus, and I-- I-- I thought I am completely immunized of that. But it wasn't true because in-- at the end of 1942, I fell sick. I survived. I remember the doctor coming to my father saying, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. It's Don't worry, Mr. Rand, your son has a heart like a horse. And that was it. [LAUGHS] And here I am. And then there was two other kind of typhus, which was called bowel typhus also abdomen typhus, and a [INAUDIBLE] typhus. And we were-- these were propagated not by lice but by sanitary conditions. And many people died of that also.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned getting bread and trading for bread. Was there any other food you had access to?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a big market. At-- most the food we got by trading things that we brought with us. You gave him a shirt, you got the bread. You get potatoes. You got all kinds of things like this. We developed there a special culinary book for-- that was no book-- how to make-- how to eat something from-- from nothing. You had polenta, all kinds of names, and all kinds of food that I don't want to see it anymore. [INAUDIBLE]-- I don't know if you know that is. That is made of corn--

speaker: interviewer
text: Cornmeal.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cornmeal. That's a Romanian kind of bread. And then when we started to work, those who worked had the right to get a kilogram or 750 grams of bread a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who provided that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It depends where you worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I-- there were a number-- there was working that had been created by the Jewish, by the Jews themselves. And I'd like to mention here name like [? Jagerndorf, ?] who made a steel factory, who activated the steel factory and the electricity there. And people worked there and got bread. And they-- by the way, they got also certificates that they shan't be deported from Mogilev-Podol'skii. I worked in two places, one which was fiction-- [INAUDIBLE] [? thief, ?] and the other one, which was real. We-- I worked in a factory for perfume. We had some captain-- in-- in-- Romanian captain-- who used to smuggle in big bottles of perfume. And we somehow got little bottles, small bottles. So we bottled it. We mixed in all kind of flavors and a little bit of water. It doesn't matter. We made it, and this has been sold to the officers of the Romanian army or to the German army. They were sending presents to-- to their wives. I don't know how much their wives liked it when they got it. But anyhow, this is one, was a given period. And then, I was referred as a worker to the-- to the German army engineers. They-- they were called Toldt Organization-- Toldt, T-O-L-D-T. And they were mostly involved in constructing bridges, railways, rail stations, highways, repairing highways, and so on. The majority of the time, I worked at the construction of two bridges over the Dniester, because when the Russians withdrew, they destroyed the bridges. So they-- I don't even know where exactly those two are the same places. But there were no bridges. We had to go over by boat, because there are no bridges that you could over.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one bridge was for railways. And the other bridge was for-- for the highway. That means for cars, for for tanks, and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we worked very hard to build up these bridges. I know that one of the bridges had 14 pillars, because the Dniester was wider-- between a kilometer and a kilometer and a half. It's close to a mile, the bridge of Dniester.

speaker: interviewer
text: So some people worked on that side of the Dniester. Some others were shipped over with little boats to the other side of the Dniester. I was very often among those who were shipped out.

speaker: interviewee
text: It is a funny little story. There was a German soldier who was usually responsible for shipping us over. There was always some soldiers with guns there. And as we started to go on the river, he used to say, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Sing the-- the Jewish song. And we sang-- we sang the HaTikvah. It was surrealistic to see those deportees, those Jews there in that situation, and the German soldiers, and he's happy that the Jews are singing HaTikvah. It was many times it happened to us. And there I worked almost until the end of the-- of the German occupation.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were still in the ghetto, do you remember any religious life, any services?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they in the open or not?

speaker: interviewee
text: But there were a number of centers. We called them [NON-ENGLISH]. One of them was in our room, where we lived in one room.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I'll tell you. We lived in one room-- my father, my mother, and in given time, my grandfather, and four children, and an aunt-- my aunt. I mean my-- my brother's-- my mother's sister. And in there, there was also a little synagogue, because we had the Sefer Torah. That's again, the Sefer Torah that did miracles there. And we had there almost daily prayers. And we had there, every Shabbat, lectures.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody know about this, any officials?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was nothing official. It was-- how to say-- we called it [NON-ENGLISH]. We organized that. And I would like to mention a name, which I enjoyed very much his lectures in [NON-ENGLISH]. And that was Rabbi Yakov Ginsberg of Gura-Humorului. Gura-Humorului is a place next to-- in the neighborhood of [INAUDIBLE]. It's spelled G-U-R-A dash H-U-M-O-R-U-L-U-I. And he was a real [HEBREW] and a wonderful pedagogue. And we liked to hear his lectures that he gave in our house to those little few people who used to come to our house. And so there were many places like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- nobody was aware of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody was aware. You didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: The--

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: People who patrolled the streets.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the streets, in the [INAUDIBLE], there was the Jewish police who called that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a Jewish government in this ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a Jewish committee.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was called-- I-- I don't remember the name. It had a Romanian name. There was the leaders of the community.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a [? militia, ?] which were the policemen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the names of the people on this committee?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know one name, which was then-- also he was tried and he got a few years, and that's called Daniloff-- D-A-N-I-L-O-F-F. And he came from [INAUDIBLE]. It were-- it was controversial, but he was brought to trial. Some people said that he used his-- his powers too much, or I don't know what is was exactly. But I don't remember the names. But I remember that there was a house for orphans, where children that were found in the streets and so on didn't know anybody, they were gathered in this house. And-- and were taken care of there. And the leading figures were two brothers, also from that Gura-Humorului, with the name of Soniu Tauber-- T-A-U-B-E-R-- and Feivish Tauber. They all were-- came-- they reached Israel afterwards. And they are not anymore around, but they passed away in Israel. They did a terrific world-- work. And in this building where there was this [NON-ENGLISH], as we called it, for orphanage, there was also a synagogue on Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur. And there, we used to go to listen to the prayers of a doctor called Dr. [? Rhode ?] who was a wonderful hazzan in addition to being a doctor. So until now, I remember certain things that I heard from that hazzan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any organizations or groups that took-- had meetings during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only organization that I remember was a general Zionist youth movement, which was underground, not official. Very few people knew about it. And we used to have certain activities in very small groups-- by two, by three, something like this-- because we didn't want to make any noise around it. We exchanged books. That was one of the activities. We sang songs. Things were-- songs were composed there in Yiddish. And I mentioned-- I would like to mention this respect-- a-- a guy who was from the [HEBREW] movement, who was the name of Tutiu Lablonower-- T-U-T-U-I-- T-U-T-U-- T-I-U I-A-B-L-O-N-O-W-E-R-- who had also a wonderful voice. He was from my place [NON-ENGLISH]. And he is-- he's still living. [LAUGHS] He's a sentimental person. And he used to compose songs there, and we used to sing it together and to dream about [HEBREW] Israel, something is a dream that we-- it was so dark around that only a little bit of light we could find in our heart, and nowhere outside. But this was a very important activity. It kept us together. And we used to also to communicate to each other when there were dangerous situations. Or something that we knew that was important to be communicated.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a kind of-- verbally, no telephone, [LAUGHS] nothing else, and not in a written way, anything. But we used from mouth to mouth. There was-- by the way, in the ghetto, there was a lot of such information going through. One heard from an officer in the German army. One heard an officer from the Romanian army. We even had two [NON-ENGLISH]. We called it [? YPA, ?] which means [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. That means the agency for Jewish gossip. And the other was called [? YVA-- ?] [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]-- Jews Walk Like This.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it doesn't matter whether it was true or not. [LAUGHS] But that's what we want. So that was news.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a part of the folklore that has been there. In this-- often or not, there were presentations-- theater presentations-- by the children, to which this-- to which we invited the authorities-- the Romanian authorities and the German authorities. And they used to come there, even these two infamous persons that everybody-- every Jew who lived in Mogilev remembers them. And that was the two-- two from the Romanian army. One called Barbelescu-- B-A-R-B-U-L-E-S-C-U, who was then tried and-- and sentenced, not to death, but a-- as a war criminal. And another was the name of D-A-N, who was the terror of the ghetto, because he had a very strong hand, and he used to hit people. And we were weak. And you couldn't hit back or something like this. These two guys they used also to come to these presentations. They also published some bulletin, in which they told about their lives. I don't have anything of these. But there is one of those children, that I know of, when she came out, she wrote a little letter to Ilya Ehrenburg in Russia, who collected letters from children after the Holocaust. And there, she appears with her little letter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was-- was there any wall or fence around this ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: There-- it wasn't a wall. But there were limits. If you went out of the ghetto, you were sentenced to death.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contact with the non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only in the market.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this market?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Mogilev, there was a market. There, they used to bring all kind of products. So that is one thing that I remember. And then they had the German, whose domain, and they were all non-Jews. But it wasn't a relationship that I became close friends with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other classes, any schooling in this ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. And that's the reason that, in addition to the fact that I never went to public school, I didn't go to any high school, either. But the only school I had there, that school of life. I learned a lot of things there, which are important. Even I learned how to cope with very difficult situations.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone else in the family work?

speaker: interviewee
text: There?

speaker: interviewer
text: In the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the camp. My sisters, no-- they were little children. My father worked occasionally. He used to sing occasionally, and people used to come, because it was a very beautiful voice, and just used to [NON-ENGLISH]. And people came in. They gave a ruble or two [? or whatsoever ?] because that was the currency there until we got Deutsch Marks. And this helped us a lot. Here and there were some people who got a chicken or something to use to slaughter it. And here and there he did some physical work. But only what he wanted, because people who were religious, in religious functions like rabbis and slaughterers, were-- did not have to work mandatory work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he able to keep his beard throughout this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Not only this-- I kept the payot all the time. It was interesting as a personal anecdote that only after I came back from-- from-- from Transnistria, and we went to Czernowitz, I decided I was ready that time-- about 18 years old, or something like this, I decided that I want to take off my payot. And I did it, and I came home for lunch. And nobody said a word to me, neither my mother, nor my father. They accepted it, probably as a sign of progress.
